learning_outcomes.description: Have a broad understanding of the role of ICT4D and community informatics in social change.
Critically evaluate the role of community informatics and ICT4D in development and social change.
Understand key factors that contribute towards, and impact on the quality and sustainability of ICTs and community informatics projects.
Have an understanding of the larger political and economic issues that impact on ICT4D in Development
Analyse and examine the relationships between media, participation and voice
Critically analyse and draft a simple  project proposal for community informatics and ICTs  for social change.

assessments.task_description:
There is a move to create a community connectivity project in a province located in country X. A local community has expressed interest in this project and is keen to begin work on mobile and internet connectivity. While a non-government organisation is willing to help them set up this project, the community is clear that it wants to be involved at all stages of the project from inception, to completion and in its everyday maintenance.

Based on your reading of the lectures and required texts on community informatics, write a 700-word response to six questions that will be shared with you before you write this test.

This is a compulsory in class test.

Texts:

Community Networks in Latin America. Available at: https://www.internetsociety.org/wp-content/uploads/2018/12/2018-Community-Networks-in-LAC-EN.pdf and

Gonzalez, R. J. (2020) Connected: How a Mexican Village Built its own cell phone network, University of California Press ( Available Online in Library)
assessments.submission_guidelines: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to defer this exam.

assessments.task_description:
Define Open Data and Open Access. What are the benefits and limitations of open data and open access? What are some of the ways in which Aaron Swartz contributed to the creation of open access? Why do some governments implement Open Data projects and others not? Using examples, explain how open data and open access can result in empowerment, access and solutions that enhance democracy and human freedoms. Provide your own reflections on the pros and cons of open data and open access.

Maximum word limit is 2000 excluding references.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Submit through the designated TurnItIn assignment on Blackboard.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.due_date:
3/09/2026 - 29/10/2026

The oral presentations will take place after the in class seminars between Weeks 6-13.
assessments.task_description:
COMU 7014 6 Minutes Oral Test

Time: 6 Minutes

Choose one project on community informatics from the list below.

Broadband for the Rural North: https://b4rn.org.uk/

Guifi.net: https://landing.guifi.net/

Zenzelini Networks: https://zenzeleni.net/our-model/

Internet Society: https://www.internetsociety.org/our-work/connectivity/community-centered-connectivity/

Routing for Community, 23 Episodes. Available at: https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PLlCNGLRvpX9pAYJ4oPB2308YPEkvk9XxR

 

1)       Introduce the project and explain why you choose this project.

2)       Define its objectives and the main work that they do.

3)       Explain how the community is involved in this project.

4)       Conclude with some reflections on the opportunities and challenges that this project faces.

Do check out YT videos on the project that you have chosen and readings that are available online. Your grade will be based on evidence of comprehension of the project, the structure of your presentation and communication skills. Overtime will invite penalties. I will circulate a presentation schedule in advance that you will be able to sign up to.


Recording of Oral and Practical Assessment 

  • All presentations will be recorded for marking purposes via recording facilities available where the assessment takes place (eg. ECHO360, Zoom, camera device)  
  • Recordings will be retained by the School of Communication and Arts for at least 12 months from the release of the final grade for the course.  
  • Recordings will be stored in a secure manner and will only be accessed by authorised school staff for the purposes of: 
  • Moderation of marking;  
  • Provision of feedback to the student(s) recorded; and/or  
  • Re-marking following a successful re-mark application.
assessments.submission_guidelines: In class oral presentation on the chosen date. A presentation roster will be circulated in advance.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

Week 1: An Introduction to Community Informatics

Introduction to the theory and practice of community informatics. This will be preceded by an introduction to the course, learning objectives, learning activities and assessment, course readings.


Learning outcomes: L01, L04

learning_activities.topic:
Week 3: e-Government: Public Sector Software and Social Change

This lecture will explore the uses of public software by governments. While e-government has its detractors snd issues with privacy and surveillance, there is a case to be made for governments using free and open software standards that enables maximum access.

Learning outcomes: L02, L03

learning_activities.topic:
Week 6: Cyberactivists and Social Change: The Case of Aaron Swartz

Aaron Swartz's life work highlights commitment to openness and access, the moral requirement that publicly funded research is made available to the public, limits to the intellectual property regime and copyright in particular. His life exemplifies some of the key objectives linked to community informatics.

Watch: The Internet's Own Boy, https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=9vz06QO3UkQ (https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=9vz06QO3UkQ)

Watch: Lessig's Laws that Choke Creativity, https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7Q25-S7jzgs (https://www.youtube.com /watch?v=7Q25-S7jzgs)

Learning outcomes: L05

learning_activities.topic:
Week 12: e-Waste

This lecture will deal with the larger consequences of electronic waste. While we would all agree that the turn towards the digital has been largely beneficial, digital plenty has its own limitations.

Learning outcomes: L01, L03

learning_activities.topic:
Week 13: Community Informatics and Social Change: Dealing with Political Economy and Context

The final lecture will deal with the larger context of community informatics, focus on the importance of structures and institutions that often determine the quality of community informatics and the need for us to take 'context' seriously.

Watch: Tractor Hacking, https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=F8JCh0owT4w (https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=F8JCh0owT4w)

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L06
